channel.title: 2. Sentence Structure on Arnesh's Website
channel.description: Recent content in 2. Sentence Structure on Arnesh's Website

item.title: 2.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content At the core of Sentence Correction is proper sentence structure, making this chapter one of the more important chapters in this course. Although the information presented here is foundational, almost all students will find either that they never learned this material or that much of it has been long forgotten. 💡MUST KNOW: Although the information presented here is foundational, almost all students will find either that they never learned this material or that much of it has been long forgotten.

item.title: 2.2 The Differences Between Phrases and Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content In language, we put words together to form two elementary types of structures: phrases and clauses. A phrase is simply a collection of words that cannot stand alone as a sentence because there is no subject-verb relationship. 💡MUST KNOW: A phrase is a collection of words without a subject-verb relationship. A phrase can never stand alone as a sentence. The following are examples of phrases: Notice that none of these phrases can stand alone as a sentence.

item.title: 2.3 Independent Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content There are two major hallmarks of an independent clause: · An independent clause contains both a subject and a finite verb. · An independent clause can stand alone as a sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: An independent clause contains a subject and a finite verb and can stand alone as a sentence. Why are independent clauses important? Well, every English sentence must have at least one independent clause, and the correct version of a sentence in a Sentence Correction question always contains at least one independent clause.

item.title: 2.4 Dependent Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content Clauses that cannot constitute sentences on their own are, quite logically, called dependent clauses. 💡MUST KNOW: Clauses that cannot constitute sentences on their own are called dependent clauses. These clauses fulfill specific functions within sentences and serve to enhance or modify the meaning expressed by an independent clause. There are three distinct types of dependent clauses: (1) Subordinate clauses (2) Relative clauses (3) Noun clauses Let’s continue by discussing subordinate clauses first.

item.title: 2.5 The Subordinate Clause
item.description: Lesson Content Consider one of our last examples of an independent clause: The crowd laughed. The clause has a subject and a finite verb - all that is needed, in this instance, for a complete sentence. Incorrect: Because the crowd laughed. Although you may not know exactly what is missing here, it’s almost certainly clear to you that we don’t have a sentence. “Because” is a member of a class of words called subordinating conjunctions, words that can take away the independence of a clause when placed at the head of the clause.

item.title: 2.6 What is a Sentence
item.description: Lesson Content In the simplest sense, a sentence is a collection of words that contains at least one independent clause. Let’s continue by talking about subjects. Most of you are familiar with the concept of the subject of a sentence. For example: Whales are highly intelligent animals. The blue ball was rolling down the hill. Doug will ask Martha to come to his house for dinner. In the sentences above, “whales,” “ball,” and “Doug” are the subjects performing the actions.

item.title: 2.7 Major Sentence Types Based on Structure
item.description: Lesson Content When talking about the structure of sentences, we divide sentences into four types: (1) Simple Sentences (2) Compound Sentences (3) Complex Sentences (4) Compound-Complex Sentences We’ll carefully discuss each of these sentence types, beginning with the simple sentence.

item.title: 2.8 The Simple Sentence
item.description: Lesson Content A simple sentence consists of a single independent clause followed by a period. 💡MUST KNOW: A simple sentence consists of a single independent clause followed by a period. The following are examples of simple sentences: I do. You will. I was at the beach. John and Jeff were reading books. Spending time with animals is one of the best ways to enjoy your days. We’ll discuss simple sentences in more detail as we progress, but for now it’s important to understand that the simple sentence is the simplest collection of words that can constitute an English sentence.

item.title: 2.9 The Compound Sentence
item.description: Lesson Content A compound sentence is composed of at least two independent clauses. 💡MUST KNOW: A compound sentence is composed of at least two independent clauses. We must connect these independent clauses in specific ways. One way is to use what’s referred to as a coordinating conjunction. There are seven coordinating conjunctions: for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so We can use the mnemonic device “FANBOYS” to help us distinguish these conjunctions from other types of transition words.

item.title: 2.10 A Semicolon Can be Used to Connect Two Independent Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content We have just discussed that one way to connect two independent clauses is by using a comma and a coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS). Consider the following example: IC IC My breakfast was cold; the door had been left open. The semicolon here acts in the same way as a comma and a coordinating conjunction would. When a semicolon is used as a substitute for a comma and coordinating conjunction, an independent clause must be found on each side of the semicolon.

item.title: 2.11 Correct Use of a Colon in a Sentence
item.description: Lesson Content We’ll encounter the colon from time to time in Sentence Correction questions. Whenever we have a colon, what precedes the colon must be able to stand on its own as a complete sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: Whenever we have a colon, what precedes the colon must be able to stand on its own as a complete sentence. For example: The board of directors made its final decision: the CEO will be replaced immediately.

item.title: 2.12 Complex Sentences
item.description: Lesson Content A complex sentence is composed of one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause (SC). 💡MUST KNOW: A complex sentence is composed of one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause. The following are examples of complex sentences: SC IC Because Lucy loves wine, I brought her a pinot noir from Burgundy. IC SC I brought Lucy a pinot noir from Burgundy because she loves wine.

item.title: 2.13 Compound-Complex Sentences
item.description: Lesson Content A compound-complex sentence is a sentence with more than one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause.For example: SC IC IC Because we went to the beach, I ran in the soft sand, so I could build my endurance. This example features one subordinate clause followed by two independent clauses, making this a compound-complex sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: A compound-complex sentence is a sentence with more than one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause.

item.title: 2.14 The Relative Clause: Another Type of Dependent Clause
item.description: Lesson Content Earlier in the chapter, we said that there are three types of dependent clauses: subordinate clauses, relative clauses, and noun clauses. Relative clauses begin with relative pronouns. that, which, who, whom, whose, where, when 💡MUST KNOW: Memorize the relative pronouns: A relative clause is a type of dependent clause, and as such, a relative clause is unable to stand alone as a sentence. Relative clauses always appear embedded within other clauses.

item.title: 2.15 Relative Clauses are Often Used to Create Sentence Fragments.
item.description: Lesson Content Remember that a relative clause is a dependent clause, and therefore it cannot stand alone as a sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: A relative clause is a dependent clause, and therefore it cannot stand alone as a sentence. In other words, a relative clause must be combined with an independent clause if we are to have a sentence. However, in Sentence Correction questions, we’ll often encounter sentence versions that are just relative clauses attached to nouns.

item.title: 2.16 Noun Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content A noun clause is a clause that plays the role of a noun. Many noun clauses begin with the following words: that, which, how, who, whom, where, whether, what, why For example, in each of the following sentences the noun clauses are bolded: Marty feels that spiritual development has no limits. That holiday leave had been cancelled depressed the entire company. The crowd was unhappy with what happened next.

item.title: 2.17 A Noun Clause Can Serve as the Subject of a Sentence.
item.description: Lesson Content Many people believe that the subject of a sentence must be a noun. That Napoleon’s ruse would fail in its intended objective had not occurred to the French generals. This is a perfectly acceptable sentence. Notice that each of the following sentences has a noun clause serving as the subject: That she had the nerve to attend the party is shocking. What few believed could exist was eventually created.

item.title: 2.18 A Noun Clause Can Serve as a Direct Object
item.description: Lesson Content We already know that a noun clause can serve as the subject of a sentence. For example: Dayo picked apples. The noun “apples” is the direct object. For example: The deer ate the green grass. The noun phrase “the green grass” is the direct object. Direct objects don’t have to be single nouns though. I knew why the deer was eating my flowers. I felt that it was time to go swimming.

item.title: 2.19 Noun Clause as Object of a Preposition
item.description: Lesson Content A noun clause can also serve as the object of a preposition. Incorrect: I was unhappy about. Incorrect: The bill was limited by. Those sets of words do not express complete thoughts. For example, a preposition can be followed by a single noun: I was unhappy about the weather. I was unhappy about the oranges. I was unhappy about market conditions. The bill was limited by greed. The bill was limited by time.

item.title: 2.20 Distinguishing Between Relative Clauses and Noun Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content The proper use of relative clauses and noun clauses is important in GMAT Sentence Correction. Since relative clauses and noun clauses look similar, when we see a clause that begins with a word such as “that,” “which,” “who,” or “where,” we need to decide whether it is a relative clause or a noun clause. It will help to remember that a relative clause must follow the noun that the clause is modifying.

item.title: 2.21 Items in a Series
item.description: Lesson Content When three or more items appear in a series, a comma and a coordinating conjunction must appear before the last item in the series. For breakfast, I had cereal, toast, and juice. Notice that, in this sentence, a comma and the coordinating conjunction “and” precede the final noun, “juice.” The comma just before the coordinating conjunction is referred to as the “Oxford Comma.” It must be present in such a list on the GMAT.

item.title: 2.22 Using Semicolons to Clarify Lists
item.description: Lesson Content As we know, a semicolon can be used between two independent clauses. Consider the following example: When Monica is in Europe, she’ll visit London, England; Paris, France; Rome, Italy; and Vienna, Austria. Notice that the sentence uses semicolons to more clearly separate items in a list that could be confusing if commas were used instead. Imagine the sentence without the semicolons: Incorrect: When Monica is in Europe, she’ll visit London, England, Paris, France, Rome, Italy, and Vienna, Austria.
